title: SEGMENT 16 — ELSI: the 5% set-aside and the ethics infrastructure

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — ELSI: the 5% set-aside and the ethics infrastructure
There is one more legacy of HGP that I have to insist on covering, because it is unique to this project and it has had effects far beyond genomics.
It is the ELSI program.
ELSI stands for Ethical, Legal, and Social Implications.

From the very beginning of the Human Genome Project, NHGRI committed a fixed percentage of its annual budget — originally three percent, later raised to five percent — to research and policy work on the ethical, legal, and social implications of genomic research.
This was unprecedented for a major federal research project.
The reasoning, articulated by Watson and then carried forward by Collins, was that genomic information is uniquely sensitive.
It is heritable.
It is shared with family members.
It can predict disease risk.
It can identify individuals uniquely.

The technologies that would be enabled by HGP — genetic testing, prenatal screening, forensic DNA, ancestry inference, employment and insurance applications — all raised serious ethical and policy questions that science alone could not answer.
The five percent set-aside funded a sustained, decades-long research program on these questions.
ELSI-funded scholars include lawyers, bioethicists, sociologists, historians, philosophers, and policy analysts as well as scientists.

ELSI funded studies on genetic discrimination, on informed consent, on privacy, on the special considerations for indigenous and historically marginalized communities, on the implications of direct-to-consumer genetic testing, on the use of forensic DNA, on prenatal and newborn screening, and on the equitable distribution of genomic medicine.
The single most concrete legislative outcome of the ELSI program was the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act, GINA, signed into U.
S. federal law in May 2008.
GINA prohibits health insurers from using genetic information to determine eligibility or premiums, and prohibits employers from using genetic information in hiring, firing, or promotion decisions.

GINA passed Congress with overwhelming bipartisan support — ninety-five to zero in the Senate, four hundred fourteen to one in the House — which is almost unheard of for any substantial piece of legislation, and it was the direct product of more than a decade of ELSI-funded research, advocacy, and coalition-building.
GINA has real limitations.
It does not cover life insurance, long-term care insurance, or disability insurance — three areas where genetic information could still legally be used against individuals.
It does not cover all employer activities, especially in small employers.
And its enforcement track record has been uneven.
But the principle that genetic information deserves special legal protection, and that society should think carefully about how genomic data is used, is now embedded in law and in policy.
That principle did not exist in 1990.
ELSI created it.
The other lasting effect of ELSI is institutional.

Almost every major genomic research project today — UK Biobank, All of Us, the Pangenome Consortium, the Population Genomics Initiative — has an ethics and community engagement component built into its design from the start.
The notion that you cannot just collect human genomic data without thinking about consent, governance, community involvement, and benefit sharing has become the standard.
That standard was set by ELSI.
This is also, by the way, a model that other emerging technology areas — AI, gene editing, synthetic biology, neurotechnology — have explicitly looked to.
The idea of dedicating a fixed budget percentage to social and ethical research alongside the technical research is now widely cited as a best practice.
Whether it is consistently implemented elsewhere is another question, but the model exists, and HGP is where it came from.
